Extremal factorization under size bounds (maximize/divisor-structure)

Let $N=973$. For a positive integer $n$, let $\tau(n)$ denote the number of positive divisors of $n$.
Let $n_*$ be the smallest integer $n$ with $1\le n\le N$ such that $\tau(n)$ is as large as possible among all integers $1\le n\le N$.
Determine $n_*$ (note that $n_*<1000$).

Solution.
Step 1 (prime_exponent_vectors): By the Fundamental Theorem of Arithmetic, every $n\ge1$ has a unique prime factorization $n=\prod_{i=1}^k p_i^{e_i}$ with primes $p_1<p_2<\cdots<p_k$ and integers $e_i\ge1$; hence $\tau(n)=\prod_{i=1}^k (e_i+1)$.
Step 2 (extremal_divisor_choice): Let $n_*$ be the smallest maximizer of $\tau(n)$ on $1\le n\le N$, and write $n_*=\prod p_i^{e_i}$ with $p_1<p_2<\cdots$. Then $e_1\ge e_2\ge\cdots\ge e_k$; otherwise for some $i<j$ with $e_i<e_j$, swapping the exponents of $p_i$ and $p_j$ produces an integer $n'<n_*$ with $\tau(n')=\tau(n_*)$ by from Step 1, contradicting minimality.
Step 3 (minimal_prime_factor_choice): For a fixed nonincreasing exponent vector $(e_1,\dots,e_k)$, the smallest integer realizing it is obtained by using the smallest primes $2,3,5,7,\dots$ in order; otherwise replacing a larger prime base by a smaller unused prime strictly decreases $n$ while preserving $\tau$ by from Step 1. Thus we may assume $n_*=2^{e_1}3^{e_2}5^{e_3}7^{e_4}\cdots$ with $e_1\ge e_2\ge\cdots$ by from Step 2.
Step 4 (inequality_bounds_for_integers): Since $2\cdot3\cdot5\cdot7\cdot11=2310>973$, any $n\le973$ has at most $4$ distinct prime factors, so $n_*=2^a3^b5^c7^d$ with integers $a\ge b\ge c\ge d\ge0$ by from Step 3. Also $2^{10}=1024>973$, so necessarily $a\le9$.
Step 5 (inequality_bounds_for_integers): For all $n\le973$ we have $\tau(n)\le32$. Indeed, if $\tau(n)>32$ then $\tau(n)\notin\{33,34,35\}$ because the minimal integers with divisor counts $33,34,35$ are $2^{10}\cdot3^2=9216$, $2^{16}\cdot3=196608$, and $2^6\cdot3^4=5184$, all $>973$ (using smallest primes as justified by from Step 3 and the divisor formula from Step 1). Hence $\tau(n)\ge36$. Let $n$ have $k$ distinct prime factors; then $1\le k\le4$ by from Step 4. If $k=4$, write $n=2^a3^b5^c7^d$ with $a\ge b\ge c\ge d\ge1$ by from Step 2, so $(a+1)(b+1)(c+1)(d+1)\ge36$ forces $(a,b,c,d)\ge(2,2,1,1)$ and therefore $n\ge2^2\cdot3^2\cdot5\cdot7=1260>973$. If $k=3$, similarly $(a+1)(b+1)(c+1)\ge36$ forces $(a,b,c)\ge(5,2,1)$ and thus $n\ge2^5\cdot3^2\cdot5=1440>973$. If $k=2$, then $(a+1)(b+1)\ge36$ forces $(a,b)\ge(8,3)$ so $n\ge2^8\cdot3^3=6912>973$. If $k=1$, then $a+1\ge36$ so $n\ge2^{35}\gg973$. All cases contradict $n\le973$, so $\tau(n)\le32$.
Step 6 (prime_exponent_vectors): The integer $840=2^3\cdot3\cdot5\cdot7$ satisfies $840\le973$ and $\tau(840)=(3+1)(1+1)(1+1)(1+1)=32$ by from Step 1, so the maximum value of $\tau(n)$ on $1\le n\le973$ is exactly $32$ by from Step 5.
Step 7 (prime_exponent_vectors): If $n\le973$ has $\tau(n)=32$, then by from Step 4 it has at most $4$ distinct primes, and by from Step 1 its exponent tuple must correspond to a factorization of $32$ into at most $4$ integers $\ge2$. Up to ordering (consistent with from Step 2), the possibilities are $(31)$, $(15,1)$, $(7,3)$, $(7,1,1)$, $(3,3,1)$, and $(3,1,1,1)$.
Step 8 (inequality_bounds_for_integers): For each exponent tuple in from Step 7, the smallest possible $n$ is obtained using primes $2,3,5,7$ by from Step 3; these minima are $2^{31}\gg973$, $2^{15}\cdot3=98304$, $2^7\cdot3^3=3456$, $2^7\cdot3\cdot5=1920$, $2^3\cdot3^3\cdot5=1080$, and $2^3\cdot3\cdot5\cdot7=840$. Only the last is $\le973$, so the only $n\le973$ with $\tau(n)=32$ is $n=840$.
Step 9 (minimal_prime_factor_choice): Since the maximal divisor count is $32$ by from Step 6, every maximizer must satisfy $\tau(n)=32$, and by from Step 8 the unique maximizer within $1\le n\le973$ is $840$; hence it is also the smallest maximizer.
Step 10 (extremal_divisor_choice): Combining from Step 5 (no $n\le973$ has more than $32$ divisors) with from Step 6 (840 attains 32) and from Step 9 (no other $n\le973$ attains 32), we conclude $n_*=840$.
Answer: 840

Uniqueness Test
The search set $\{1,2,\dots,N\}$ is finite, so a maximum of $\tau(n)$ exists, and the tie-break rule "choose the smallest maximizer" enforces a single output. In this instance, from Step 6 the maximum value is $32$, and from Step 8 the unique integer $n\le973$ attaining $\tau(n)=32$ is $840$, so the output is uniquely determined as $n_*=840$.

840
